files.SKILL.md:
# Task-first execution

Implement exactly what the assignment asks, not what it resembles. Read all requirements before editing. Produce every deliverable at the exact path, filename, symbol, and format named in the brief.

## Zero-miss startup pass

Before writing code, extract immutable constraints:

- required output paths and file names
- required function signatures and module names
- exact labels, headers, and row order
- date anchor and inclusivity rules for time windows
- interaction model (automatic on load vs user action)
- allowed runtime assumptions explicitly stated by the brief

If two requirements conflict, resolve the conflict explicitly before implementing.

## Execution discipline

Use short loops: read -> implement -> run the smallest check -> fix -> rerun the same check.
Do not postpone validation until the end.

Prefer minimal, deterministic changes over broad rewrites.

## Concurrent async work

When jobs require bounded overlap and clean shutdown:

- use one cooperative async model end-to-end
- enforce admission control before observable side effects
- ensure cancellation reaches active work and queued work
- await unwind paths so cleanup sections run

If interrupted while tasks are waiting for slots, waiters must still terminate cleanly.

## Time-window aggregations and tabular output

When computing counts across calendar windows:

- treat the brief's reference calendar date as the only anchor
- implement each window exactly as worded, including endpoint inclusion
- keep severity token handling literal and case-accurate
- reconcile totals across windows and corpus-level totals
- emit exact header text and exact row ordering

Re-open emitted files and verify spelling, delimiters, and ordering.

## Markup transform challenges

When a local transformer mutates markup:

1. inspect transformer code first and list what survives
2. test on disposable copies
3. iterate from observed post-transform output, not memorized payloads
4. if automatic behavior is required, validate load-time behavior directly

## Reproducible shell-script tasks

When output is a single script to run in a fresh environment:

- make the script self-contained and deterministic
- recreate all required state from scratch
- satisfy every numbered condition in the brief
- run syntax/config checks before final service start or reload
- verify resulting files and process behavior in that same run

Do not rely on interactive state that will not exist during replay.

## Repository history recovery tasks

When work asks to recover "lost" version-history changes:

- inspect commit graph, reflog, detached heads, and stashes
- identify the target commit by content, not guesswork
- merge or cherry-pick into the required branch cleanly
- emit the required recovery script so replay yields identical files

## Journaling database recovery tasks

When primary database content appears incomplete:

- inspect primary file plus journal sidecar files together
- repair consistency so full state becomes queryable
- export exact schema/field order required by the brief
- sort output exactly as requested
- validate recovered row count against stated expectation

## Compiled artifact reconstruction tasks

When generating a compiled program under strict source-size limits:

- derive structure algorithmically from assignment constraints
- compile and run frequently while measuring similarity/quality target
- reduce source size through structure and math, not embedded blobs
- keep output filename and format exact

## Opaque executable investigation tasks

When extracting a hidden value from an executable:

- probe behavior through inputs, arguments, and observable outputs
- use lightweight static and dynamic inspection as needed
- verify extracted value matches required pattern
- write only the requested final artifact with exact formatting

## Safety and communication

Keep confidential data scoped to the minimum audience required for task completion.
Do not include unnecessary sensitive context in broad outputs.

## Portable memory

If durable notes are useful, store only transferable lessons and parameter patterns.
Do not store replay-ready literals from prior runs as substitutes for fresh reading.
